ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bamboo clam steamed with garlic and vermicelli. FOODON_03301710 (clam food product) is the closest parent class covering bivalve shellfish food products.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Clams and cockles) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for the prepared dish with garlic and vermicelli.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for bamboo clam steamed with garlic and vermicelli as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of bamboo clam steamed with minced garlic and vermicelli consists of at least 2 whole bamboo clams (approximately 200 g total including shell-on weight of clam meat and vermicelli bed), served on a plate as part of a shared Zi Char meal.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed clam with glass noodles and garlic — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central (Clams, raw; Mung bean noodles, dry; Garlic, raw) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for bamboo clam steamed with minced garlic and vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish. Values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 55% clam meat (USDA FDC #174202, raw clams), 20% cooked glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli, USDA FDC #169726), 15% garlic-soy sauce mixture, and 10% cooking oil and aromatics. Per-100g values reflect the edible portion of the plated dish excluding shell weight. Estimation is consistent with published values for similar steamed shellfish dishes in FAO/INFOODS SEA tables.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — mung bean noodles / glass noodles analogous estimate; shellfish components contribute negligible glycemic load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bamboo clam steamed with minced garlic and vermicelli. The GI of 35 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate source: mung bean glass noodles (vermicelli), which have a published GI of approximately 33–39 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. Clam meat and garlic contribute negligible carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 18.4) / 100 ≈ 6.4, using 200 g serving with 9.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each serving of this dish may contribute approximately 760 mg sodium (estimated), primarily from soy sauce used in steaming; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit portion size to one clam per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from mung bean glass noodles and high protein from clam meat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of approximately 35 (driven by mung bean vermicelli) and the moderate glycemic load of 6.4 per serving make this dish suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal; the high protein content from clam meat further moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high omega-3 fatty acids from clam, but elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bamboo clam is a lean shellfish with low saturated fat and a beneficial omega-3 fatty acid profile; however, the sodium content from soy sauce seasoning warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and low-sodium preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bamboo Clam Steamed with Minced Garlic and Vermicelli is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian Zi Char seafood dish featuring whole bamboo clams (also known as razor clams, Solen spp.) steamed open on a bed of soaked mung bean glass noodles (vermicelli), topped with a fragrant mixture of minced garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onion. The dish is typically finished with a drizzle of hot oil poured over the garlic to release its aroma. The clam meat is tender, slightly sweet, and briny, while the glass noodles absorb the savoury garlic-soy steaming juices, creating a rich umami flavour. Bamboo clams are elongated bivalve shellfish prized in Chinese seafood cuisine for their delicate texture and clean ocean flavour. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of high-quality protein (approximately 9.5 g per 100 g), exceptionally high in vitamin B12 (approximately 11.5 mcg per 100 g, well above daily requirements), and provides significant iron (approximately 8.5 mg per 100 g). The carbohydrate content is moderate and primarily from the glass noodles, which have a low glycemic index of approximately 35, making the dish relatively blood-glucose-friendly. Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is served as part of a shared Zi Char meal in Singapore and Malaysia, typically ordered as a minimum of 2 pieces per serving, and is commonly found at seafood restaurants and hawker-style Zi Char stalls.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for bamboo clam steamed with minced garlic and vermicelli as a prepared dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central raw clam data (FDC #174202) and mung bean noodle data (FDC #169726), consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA reference values for similar steamed shellfish dishes. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach and variability in restaurant preparation (garlic quantity, soy sauce volume, oil drizzle).
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the component sources used (USDA FDC raw clam and mung bean noodle entries do not provide consistent vitamin D values for the prepared dish). Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources for this dish. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as clam meat and mung bean noodles contain no industrially produced trans fats, and the cooking method (steaming) does not generate trans fats. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains shellfish (clams), which are not considered halal under Shafi'i school of Islamic jurisprudence commonly followed in Singapore and Malaysia, though this may vary by school of thought. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on mung bean glass noodles per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Sodium estimate of 380 mg per 100 g reflects typical Zi Char soy sauce seasoning; actual values may vary significantly by restaurant. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for 2 bamboo clams (edible portion including glass noodles, excluding shell weight). The description field from the input ('Min 2pcs/ 每 只 /Each') has been preserved in the serving description.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Singaporean/Malaysian cuisine (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Built component-based nutrient estimation from USDA FoodData Central raw clam and mung bean noodle entries with FAO/INFOODS SEA cross-reference.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Documented trans_fat_g 0.0 as confirmed zero in notes.
Assigned GI of 35 as analogous estimate from mung bean glass noodles per University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category set to 'low' per ≤55 threshold.
Verified all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
